evidence: goseg/web/_app/immutable/chunks/websocket.84e20bf3.js centralizes transport, state stores, reconnect behavior, and dozens of domain command exports in one module (`let m=new Set,p;`, `setTimeout(()=>M(t),1e3)`, many `e({type:...})` wrappers).

goseg/web/_app/immutable/chunks/urbit.f551ce98.js uses a process-wide singleton client (`const f=new v("")`) and mutates global callbacks/subscriptions (`f.onOpen`, `f.onRetry`, `f.onError`, `f.subscribe(...)`) from exported functions.
Multiple entry pages directly import websocket transport chunk (`apps-page`, `boot-existing-page`, `captive-page`, `system-page`) rather than consuming a narrower domain boundary.

ui/src/lib/stores/logsocket.js introduces a second independent websocket lifecycle (`let SESSION = {};`, `new WebSocket(url)`, per-type handlers), duplicating connection-state concerns outside the main websocket hub.

`ui/src/lib/stores/websocket-commands.js` exposes a single factory returning ~80 command functions, each stringly assembling protocol payloads (`type`, `action`, arbitrary fields), which creates a wide bag API instead of domain-scoped interfaces.
The same command surface mixes unrelated domains (auth, setup, urbit lifecycle, support, dev) in one module, so callers depend on one oversized abstraction boundary.

`ui/src/lib/stores/data.js` defaults `URBIT_MODE` to `true` while `ui/vite.config.js` computes `GS_URBIT_MODE`/`GS_DEV_PANEL` from environment and compiled layout code sets mode flags during mount, creating a split contract for mode state ownership.

`goseg/web/_app/immutable/entry/_layout.svelte.8a290471.js` combines routing decisions, auth gate choreography, websocket initialization, and periodic timers (`setTimeout` loops) inside one boundary component.

`goseg/web/_app/immutable/chunks/urbit.f551ce98.js` uses a mutable singleton transport (`const f = new v("")`) with global callbacks (`onOpen`, `onRetry`, `onError`) that are reconfigured by UI entrypoints, making handoffs lifecycle-sensitive.

Layout mount path directly chooses transport setup path (`ce(window.ship)` vs websocket connect) and also mutates mode stores, so startup behavior depends on implicit cross-module ordering rather than explicit orchestrator seams.

holistic_context.structure.directory_profiles shows `goseg/web/_app/immutable/chunks/` with 49 generated files and `goseg/web/_app/immutable/entry/` with 31 generated files; assigned files use hashed build-artifact names like `0.f72efb1a.js`, `10.35bd59a0.js`, `(home)-page.svelte.3cdaa764.js`.

Assigned chunk/entry files are one-line compiled outputs that import other hashed compiled modules (for example `chunks/0.f72efb1a.js` -> `entry/_layout.svelte.8a290471.js`), indicating build products are organized as first-class source tree content rather than isolated artifact output.

holistic_context.structure shows `ui/src/lib/stores/` has 17 files (>10 threshold), while assigned files in that folder mix unrelated concerns: global app flags and date helper in `data.js`, isolated dev-only class store in `devclass.js`, and colocated unit test `data.test.js`.
README.md has installation/build instructions but no required CI quality gate or test command for frontend networking/auth pathways.

goseg/web/_app/immutable/chunks/websocket.84e20bf3.js performs critical behaviors (token storage, reconnect, command dispatch, JSON parsing) without any test references or contract assertions in the reviewed governance docs.

goseg/web/_app/immutable/chunks/urbit.f551ce98.js includes event-stream parsing/retry/ack logic and network side effects, but reviewed files provide no documented integration test policy for these paths.

Command API contract diverges between source and shipped bundle: `bootShip(patp, key, keyType, remote, selectedDrive)` in `ui/src/lib/stores/websocket-commands.js` sends `{ keyType, key }`, while bundled `lt=(t,a,o,s)=>...{key:a,remote:o,selectedDrive:s}` in `goseg/web/_app/immutable/chunks/websocket.84e20bf3.js` omits `keyType`.

Source exposes commands such as `startramCancel`, `setPenpaiCores`, `removePenpai`, `resetSetup`, and `devRestoreTlon` in `ui/src/lib/stores/websocket-commands.js`, but the exported command set in `goseg/web/_app/immutable/chunks/websocket.84e20bf3.js` does not include equivalent handlers, indicating inconsistent API surfaces by build target.

`ui/src/lib/stores/logsocket.js` marks `connect`/`requestLogs` as `async` but they are fire-and-forget side-effect APIs with no meaningful resolved value or error contract; callers cannot tell when websocket readiness or request send actually succeeds.

`createWebsocketCommands` in `ui/src/lib/stores/websocket-commands.js` aggregates authentication, setup, system power/network, StarTram, upload, ship lifecycle, support, logs, Penpai, and dev/test toggles in one monolithic factory, indicating mixed responsibilities in a single module.

The shipped generated counterpart `goseg/web/_app/immutable/chunks/websocket.84e20bf3.js` contains a similarly broad flat command set, so the same mixed-concern structure is propagated into runtime artifacts.

`daysUntilDate` in `ui/src/lib/stores/data.js` uses `Math.abs(targetDate - currentDate)`, so past and future dates both return non-negative values despite the 'until' naming implying directional semantics; `ui/src/lib/stores/data.test.js` only asserts non-negative output, reinforcing the ambiguous contract.

findings.summary: A single websocket hub module owns transport, state, retries, and broad domain command dispatch, creating a high-blast-radius dependency center.
evidence: websocket.84e20bf3.js maintains module-global connection state (`let m=new Set,p`) and reconnect policy (`p.onclose=()=>R(t)`, `setTimeout(()=>M(t),1e3)`).
The same module exports a very large command surface (`e({type:"system"...})`, `e({type:"urbit"...})`, `e({type:"support"...})`, etc.), combining unrelated domains behind one runtime hub.
Entry pages import this same chunk directly, coupling UI route modules to transport internals.
findings.suggestion: Split websocket concerns into a transport client plus domain-specific action modules (system, urbit, support, setup), and expose a thin application service boundary for pages so route modules depend on domain services instead of the transport hub.

findings.summary: Global singleton event clients introduce hidden cross-module coupling and lifecycle side effects.
evidence: urbit.f551ce98.js instantiates a singleton at module scope (`const f=new v("")`) and stores mutable handlers/subscriptions on it (`f.onOpen`, `f.onRetry`, `f.onError`, `f.subscribe`).
Class `v` constructor registers browser lifecycle behavior (`window.addEventListener("beforeunload", this.delete)`), tying network client behavior to global page lifecycle.

websocket.84e20bf3.js imports and conditionally routes through this singleton path (`import {b as w} from "./urbit..."`, `if(S(f)) w(t)`), making call behavior depend on global runtime flags and shared singleton state.
findings.suggestion: Replace module-level singleton clients with injected client instances owned by an explicit connection manager; make subscription setup/teardown explicit per consumer and avoid constructor-time global event binding.

findings.summary: Two separate websocket stacks duplicate connection lifecycle logic and fragment architectural boundaries.
evidence: ui/src/lib/stores/logsocket.js owns its own session map and lifecycle (`let SESSION = {};`, `connect`, `disconnect`, `requestLogs`, `new WebSocket(url)`), independent from the main websocket client.
websocket.84e20bf3.js independently handles connection setup, reconnect, request routing, and state updates for broader app traffic.
Both modules implement overlapping transport responsibilities with different state models, increasing drift and making boundary ownership unclear.
findings.suggestion: Unify websocket lifecycle under one transport subsystem with typed channels/streams (including logs) so modules share retry/auth/session policy and expose only domain-level APIs to stores/pages.

findings.summary: Single mega command factory creates a low-leverage, stringly typed abstraction boundary.
evidence: `createWebsocketCommands` returns a very large object of command helpers spanning many domains; almost every helper just forwards hardcoded action strings and ad-hoc payload shapes.
Boot UI bundle code is correspondingly forced to orchestrate many stage-specific command paths rather than consuming a smaller domain API, indicating the abstraction boundary is too broad.
findings.suggestion: Split `createWebsocketCommands` into domain modules (e.g., `authCommands`, `systemCommands`, `urbitCommands`, `supportCommands`) with typed payload builders and shared schema validation. Export a composed facade only where needed, and keep feature modules importing only their domain command set.

findings.summary: Mode flags are owned by conflicting abstractions (store defaults vs build-time env injection).
evidence: `data.js` initializes `URBIT_MODE` as `true` and `DEV_PANEL` as `false`, while `vite.config.js` injects `GS_URBIT_MODE` and `GS_DEV_PANEL` from env.
Compiled layout initializes and sets mode stores during mount, meaning runtime mode truth is split across static defaults, build defines, and entrypoint mutation.
findings.suggestion: Create one explicit mode-config module that reads injected env once and exports immutable runtime config; initialize stores from that module (not hardcoded defaults) and remove duplicate ownership paths.

findings.summary: Startup sequencing is tightly coupled across layout, routing, and singleton transport state.
evidence: Layout code runs recurring timer loops for auth/routing decisions and directly triggers transport setup, while also handling navigation redirects.
Transport module keeps mutable singleton state and callbacks; UI entrypoint mutates this global object, so behavior depends on which component mounts/configures it first.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a dedicated session-orchestrator service that owns startup state machine (auth check, transport connect, routing intent) and exposes explicit events/state to layout. Remove mutable singleton callback reassignment from UI code by passing dependencies through a constructed client instance.

findings.summary: Generated Svelte build artifacts are organized as stable package structure, obscuring ownership boundaries.
evidence: All assigned files are hashed/minified outputs (single-line compiled JS) under `goseg/web/_app/immutable/*`, not human-owned modules.

File naming and imports encode build fingerprinting (`*.443ba7fd.js`, `*.35bd59a0.js`) and cross-reference other fingerprinted files, which creates unstable path ownership and poor navigability for architectural reasoning.
holistic_context.structure marks both `chunks/` and `entry/` directories as fully `generated`, confirming directory boundaries represent build internals rather than domain boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create a dedicated artifact root such as `goseg/web/dist/_app/immutable/` (or keep out of repo entirely) and reserve `goseg/web/src/` for authored code; (2) move current `goseg/web/_app/immutable/chunks/*` then `goseg/web/_app/immutable/entry/*` into the artifact root; (3) update static serving/build config to point to the new artifact root and add ignore rules for hashed outputs if they should not be versioned; (4) run import/build validation commands: `cd goseg/web && npm run build`, `npm run test` (or project equivalent), and `rg "_app/immutable" goseg/web` to verify no stale paths remain.

findings.summary: The stores package is overloaded with mixed concerns, reducing clear placement boundaries.
evidence: `data.js` combines multiple global state stores with a generic date utility (`daysUntilDate`) that is not store-specific.
`devclass.js` is a tiny dev-only writable export in the same broad stores folder, indicating weak sub-package boundaries for environment-specific vs shared runtime state.

holistic_context.structure.directory_profiles reports `ui/src/lib/stores/` has 17 files (>10 mixed-concern threshold), suggesting flat-folder overload where placement no longer signals ownership cleanly.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) create focused folders `ui/src/lib/stores/runtime/`, `ui/src/lib/stores/dev/`, and `ui/src/lib/utils/date/`; (2) move `devclass.js` to `stores/dev/devclass.js` and split `daysUntilDate` from `data.js` into `utils/date/daysUntilDate.js`; (3) keep `data.js` for state exports only (or rename to a domain-specific store module) and move `data.test.js` beside the extracted helper/store it validates; (4) run import-update and validation commands: `cd ui && rg "from './data|stores/devclass|daysUntilDate' src`, update paths, then run `npm run test` and `npm run build` to confirm boundary-safe moves.

findings.summary: Transport, auth persistence, and domain command orchestration are collapsed into one websocket boundary consumed directly by pages.
evidence: websocket chunk defines connection/reconnect behavior (`new WebSocket`, `setTimeout(()=>M(t),1e3)`), token persistence (`localStorage.setItem`), and a large command fan-out (`type:"system"|"urbit"|"startram"|...`) in the same module.
apps-page and captive-page import command exports from that same module, tying UI components to a broad low-level runtime surface instead of narrower per-domain interfaces.
findings.suggestion: Split runtime boundaries into staged modules: (1) create `ui/src/lib/runtime/transport/{websocket,sse}.ts` for pure connection lifecycle and parsing, (2) create `ui/src/lib/runtime/session/token-store.ts` for credential persistence policy, (3) create `ui/src/lib/runtime/commands/{system,urbit,startram,support}.ts` that depend on transport interfaces, (4) have pages import only domain command facades. Validate by running `npm test`, `npm run build`, and smoke-testing login/reconnect flows after regeneration.

findings.summary: The Urbit SSE client mixes protocol adapter logic with GroundSeg-specific app semantics, blurring cross-module contracts.
evidence: urbit chunk contains generic event-stream parser/retry state and app-specific calls (`app:"groundseg"`, `/broadcast`, heartbeat poke) in the same class/module.
websocket chunk conditionally delegates to Urbit path (`if(S(f)) w(t)`) without an explicit adapter contract layer, creating implicit mode switching between transport systems.
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit adapter contracts in stages: (1) define a shared `RealtimeClient` interface in `ui/src/lib/runtime/contracts/realtime.ts`, (2) implement `UrbitSseClient` and `NativeWebSocketClient` adapters separately, (3) move GroundSeg event routing into `ui/src/lib/runtime/handlers/groundseg-events.ts`, (4) bind selection logic in a small composition root. Validate by running adapter-level tests and end-to-end subscription/poke regression tests before shipping generated assets.

findings.summary: Critical connection/auth runtime paths have no documented quality gate in project governance docs.
evidence: README build section only states to run `build.sh` and contains no mandatory test or CI gate for frontend runtime behavior.
websocket chunk includes high-risk behaviors (token persistence, reconnect loops, command dispatch, JSON parse of server payloads).
urbit chunk includes event-stream parsing and retry/ack control flow, but reviewed governance files do not define coverage or regression requirements for these paths.
findings.suggestion: Add a staged quality-gate policy: (1) in README, add required commands `npm test` and integration test target before release; (2) add integration tests for login/reconnect/token and SSE ack/retry flows in `ui/src/lib/stores/*.test.js` or a dedicated `ui/src/lib/runtime/__tests__/`; (3) wire CI to fail build if those tests fail; (4) add a release checklist entry requiring generated bundle refresh only after tests pass.

findings.summary: Large generated hashed frontend artifacts are treated as primary runtime files, obscuring source ownership and package boundaries.
evidence: holistic structure reports `goseg/web/_app/immutable/chunks/` with 49 generated files and `goseg/web/_app/immutable/entry/` with 31 generated files.
Assigned operational/runtime files are hashed generated outputs, not source-located modules, making package ownership and change intent hard to trace from docs to code.
README lacks a package map clarifying source folders vs generated deliverables, so reviewers encounter generated bundles as de facto architecture artifacts.
findings.suggestion: Execute a staged reorg plan: Stage 1 create explicit source/runtime split (`ui/src/` as source of truth, `goseg/web/_app/immutable/` as build-output target). Stage 2 move any manually maintained runtime logic out of generated tree into `ui/src/lib/runtime/{transport,commands,contracts}` and stop editing generated files. Stage 3 update build pipeline to publish generated assets only in release artifacts (or clearly isolated output dir) and add README package map. Stage 4 update imports in source modules and validate with `rg "_app/immutable" ui/src`, `npm run build`, and `npm test`.

findings.summary: Governance documentation describes operations but not package-level runtime contract ownership, leaving docs and code layout misaligned.
evidence: README focuses on installation/uninstall/channel selection and does not define frontend package boundaries or compatibility ownership for pages/transport modules.
Entry pages consume generated chunk APIs directly, but package-level contract ownership is not documented where contributors would expect it.
findings.suggestion: Use a staged doc+layout reorg: Stage 1 add `docs/architecture/frontend-runtime.md` describing package boundaries (`ui/src/lib/runtime`, `ui/src/routes`, generated output policy). Stage 2 add README links to that contract doc and a source-of-truth statement. Stage 3 move any contract-relevant notes currently implied by generated files into source-adjacent docs (`ui/src/lib/runtime/README.md`). Stage 4 run `npm run build && npm test` plus `rg "from \"../chunks/websocket" ui/src` to ensure source imports no longer rely on generated entry/chunk paths.

findings.summary: The websocket command API differs between source and bundled runtime code, creating inconsistent payload contracts.
evidence: `websocket-commands.js` defines `bootShip(patp, key, keyType, remote, selectedDrive)` and sends `{ keyType, key, remote, selectedDrive }`.
Bundled `websocket.84e20bf3.js` defines `lt=(t,a,o,s)=>...{type:"new_ship",action:"boot",patp:t,key:a,remote:o,selectedDrive:s}`, omitting `keyType`.

Source command surface includes functions absent from bundled exports (for example `startramCancel`, `setPenpaiCores`, `removePenpai`, `resetSetup`, `devRestoreTlon`), indicating two non-equivalent public command interfaces.
findings.suggestion: Generate both development and production command APIs from a single typed command manifest (action name + required fields), and add a build-time parity test that fails when source command definitions and bundled command payload signatures diverge.

findings.summary: Socket-facing APIs expose inconsistent async shapes and missing readiness/error contracts.
evidence: `connect` and `requestLogs` in `logsocket.js` are declared `async` but do not define success/failure semantics for callers; errors are logged to console, not surfaced.
`requestLogs` uses `SESSION[logType].send(...)` without verifying `SESSION[logType]` exists and is open, so caller-visible behavior depends on timing and hidden global state.
Command-sending entrypoints in bundled websocket code (`e(...)`) are also fire-and-forget and do not return structured command acknowledgements, despite separate ack handling existing internally.
findings.suggestion: Define a uniform command/send contract that returns a promise resolving/rejecting on ack or transport failure, and enforce socket readiness checks before send; expose these guarantees consistently in both log socket and command socket APIs.

findings.summary: A single command factory centralizes many unrelated domains, creating a high-change blast radius and weak cohesion.
evidence: `createWebsocketCommands` includes auth, setup, system, startram, upload, ship lifecycle, logs, support, penpai, and dev actions in one file and one returned object.
This file serves as a catch-all integration point for operational and developer-only behaviors (`resetSetup`, `printMounts`, backup/restore dev helpers), rather than domain-focused modules.
Bundled websocket chunk mirrors this flat action sprawl, confirming the design issue is systemic rather than local to one source file.
findings.suggestion: Split command construction by domain (`authCommands`, `systemCommands`, `urbitCommands`, `supportCommands`, `devCommands`) and compose them through a small registry with shared send helpers; keep dev-only commands in a separate module/feature gate.

findings.summary: The day-difference helper and its test encode an ambiguous contract that hides directionality.
evidence: `daysUntilDate` computes `Math.abs(targetDate - currentDate)`, which treats past and future dates identically.
The only test asserts non-negative output, so the current behavior is effectively 'absolute day distance' while the function name implies 'until' semantics.
This mismatch pushes temporal interpretation burden to callers and increases risk of subtly wrong reminder/countdown logic.
findings.suggestion: Either rename the helper to reflect absolute distance (for example `daysFromDate`) or change implementation to signed/forward-only semantics and add tests for past, present, and future edge cases (including timezone boundaries).
